segments.s111-g25: بر (preposition) + نیستان (locative noun) gives the setting: 'upon the reed‑bed.' بستر خواب is a noun phrase where بستر 'bed' is specified by خواب 'sleep' (a genitive/attributive relationship: 'a bed for sleeping'). ساخت is a simple past tense verb (3rd singular) meaning 'made/constructed' and completes the clause.
segments.s111-g26: در بیم functions as an adverbial phrase 'in (case of) fear/danger.' را here is the definite/object/topic marker highlighting what is being judged. جای ایمن is the noun phrase 'a safe place,' and شناخت is used as a verb in the past tense 'he recognized/considered' (poetic compression: object/topic marked by را + predicate 'جای ایمن' after it).
segments.s111-g27: دران is a fused locative/adverbial 'in that' (در + آن) setting the clause. نیستان names the place 'reed‑bed.' بیشه شیر is a nominal phrase 'a thicket/grove of lions' (بیشه head noun modified by شیر), and بود is the past copula 'there was.'
segments.s111-g28: که introduces a relative clause modifying بیشه; the clause that follows is highly compressed and poetic. پیلی is used with the sense 'elephant(ine)' and نیارست appears here in a nominal/poetic role (treated as 'companion' or related nominal), while ازو 'from it' and نی درود 'reed‑greeting' form a terse image ('greeting from the reed'). Grammatically, the clause is a relative/qualifying remark, with prepositional/nominal phrases packed for poetic effect.
segments.s111-g29: چو is the conjunction 'when.' یک پاس here is a short measure/nominal phrase ('one pass' / 'one guard/passer') and بگذشت is the past tense verb 'passed' (3rd singular). درنده شیر is a noun phrase 'the ferocious/fierce lion' with the adjective درنده qualifying شیر.
segments.s111-g30: به سوی is a prepositional phrase 'toward,' کنام خود is a possessive locative 'his lair/bosom' (خود emphatic/possessive here), آمد is the past tense verb 'came,' and دلیر is an epithet 'brave' appended as a predicate/adjectival comment on the subject (poetic word order places it after the verb).
segments.s111-g31: بر نی is the locative 'on/over the reed.' یکی پیل را is an object phrase 'one elephant' with را marking it as definite/object. خفته is a past participle/adjectival 'asleep,' and دید is the simple past verb 'he saw.' Word order is subject/object/verb typical for narrative past in poetry.
segments.s111-g32: بر او here means 'beside/over it' (referring back to the elephant). یکی اسپ is 'a certain horse' (one horse), آشفته is an adjective 'disturbed/panicked,' and دید is the past verb 'he saw.' The clause places the location first (بر او) then the observed entity, a common poetic order.
segments.s111-g33: نخست is an initial adverb meaning “first.” اسپ را is the direct object marked by را (he addresses the horse). گفت is the past tense ‘he said’ (here introducing reported speech) and the clause’s content follows without an explicit conjunction (که) which is often omitted in classical Persian. باید شکست is a short predicate: باید = ‘must/necessary’ and شکست functions as the notion ‘be broken / be tamed’ (verbal noun/short passive idea) — together ‘must be broken.’
segments.s111-g34: چو here = “when.” خواهم is an auxiliary/verb meaning ‘I will / I wish’; سوارم is an archaic 1st‑person form meaning ‘I mount / I get on (the horse)’ (the -م is the 1sg clitic). The clause means “when I will mount.” خود = “itself/himself” (emphatic/reflexive), آید is present/future ‘comes,’ and به دست literally ‘to (my) hand’ — a set phrase meaning ‘under my control / at my command.’
segments.s111-g35: سوی is a preposition ‘toward.’ رخش is the steed’s name (or ‘steed’) and رخشان is an epithet ‘radiant’ (adjective after the noun). برآمد is the past ‘rose / arose / came up.’ دمان (poetic variant of دَمَـن) here means ‘hem / skirt’ (used metaphorically — the poet can use clothing terms for flowing parts like mane or flank). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the prepositional phrase precedes the verb and the noun دمان follows the verb as the thing that rose.
segments.s111-g36: چو = ‘when.’ آتش بجوشید is a figurative clause: literally ‘fire boiled,’ i.e. something (here the horse) surged/seethed like fire — بجوشید is past ‘boiled / seethed / gushed.’ رخش following the verb names the thing that so behaved (the horse), and آن زمان ‘at that time / then’ is a temporal adverbial. The line uses vivid metaphor: the horse is likened to boiling fire.
segments.s111-g37: دو دست = ‘both hands’ (literally ‘two hands’ used idiomatically). اندر آورد is an archaic verbal phrase ‘brought in / put in / seized’ — here ‘he seized (with) both hands.’ و زد = ‘and he struck’ (زد is past of زدن). بر سرش = ‘upon his head’ (بر + سر + enclitic ـش = his) marks the place struck. Subject is omitted (typical in Persian) and understood from context.
segments.s111-g38: همان = ‘the same / that very.’ تیز دندان = ‘sharp tooth / fang’ (تیز qualifies دندان). به پشت اندرش is a locative phrase: به = ‘into / to,’ پشت = ‘back,’ اندر + ش (اندرش) = ‘in his’ (اندر is an older preposition ‘in/within’ and ـش = his). Together ‘the sharp fang into his back’ (i.e. the fang sank into his back).
segments.s111-g39: همی زد is an iterative/habitual past — ‘he kept striking’ (همی marks continuous or repeated action). بران خاک = ‘upon that ground / on that spot’ (بران = بر + آن / ‘thereon’). تا introduces the extent/result clause ‘until,’ and پاره کرد = ‘he tore (it/him) to pieces’ (پاره as ‘torn’ plus کردن ‘to make/do’ = ‘tore’).
segments.s111-g40: ددی را = the demon (ددی is a poetic/archaic form for an evil creature) as direct object (را marks definiteness). بران چاره = ‘by that / from that remedy’ (بران = ‘thereby/on that,’ چاره = ‘remedy/means’); بیچاره کرد = ‘made (him) helpless / miserable’ — literal ‘made (him) without a remedy.’ The whole means ‘he thereby left the demon without any way out.’
segments.s111-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by means of' introducing the instrument/source; پیکان (arrow) and تیرآتشی (fire-arrow) form a noun phrase specifying the weapon; برفروخت is a past tense verb here meaning 'kindled/ set on fire' (not 'sold'). Word order is poetic: preposition + instrument phrase + verb.
segments.s111-g18: بدو is a dative/locative 'to it' (referring back to the blaze); خاک، خاشاک، هیزم are a paratactic list of items ('earth, chaff, firewood') followed by the verb بسوخت, an intransitive/passive-form past 'was burned' (prefix بـ indicates inchoative/passive use). The verb governs the whole list, so the sequence reads 'to it earth, chaff and firewood burned.'
segments.s111-g19: بران is an adverbial/temporal particle 'thereupon' or 'on that'; آتش تیز is a noun + adjective 'keen/fierce fire'; بریانش is بریان + enclitic -ش (the third-person singular object pronoun, 'him/it'), so 'roasted him/seared him'; کرد is the past-tense causative 'made/did' used with بریانش to mean 'caused him to be roasted.'
segments.s111-g20: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after'); که introduces the result/clause that follows ('that/so that'); بی‌پوست and بی‌جانش are predicative compounds meaning 'without skin' and 'without life' respectively, with بی‌جانش carrying the clitic -ش ('him'); کرد here functions as 'caused' — i.e. 'it caused him to be skinless and lifeless.'
segments.s111-g21: بخورد is a simple past 'he ate'; the conjunction و links to the next verb بینداخت 'he threw/cast away' (past). زو is the contracted poetic form z + او = 'from it/of it' marking source of the object استخوان 'bones'. The sequence thus reads 'he ate and threw away the bones from it.'
segments.s111-g22: همین is the demonstrative 'this/that same'; بود is the past copula 'was'; the construction repeats in parallel ('this was X and this was Y') — دیگ 'cauldron' and خوان 'table/banquet' are equated to the foregoing action/results. The symmetric structure emphasizes that what remained served as his cauldron and feast.
segments.s111-g23: لگام 'bridle' is the direct object; از سرِ رخش is a prepositional phrase 'from the head of Rakhsh' (سر = 'head' attached to the horse's name رخش); برداشت is the past tense 'he removed/took off.' خوار appears postverbally as an adjective 'despised/worthless' modifying the (implied) subject or characterizing the actor; its postverbal placement is a poetic option for emphasis.
segments.s111-g24: چرا is here the archaic verbal sense 'to graze' (not 'why'); چرا دید thus means 'he saw (it) grazing' where چرا functions as a verb/gerundive notion and دید is the past 'he saw.' و links to بگذاشت 'he let/left (it)' — بگذاشت frequently means 'allowed' or 'left in place.' در مرغزار is a locative phrase 'in the meadow.'
segments.s111-g49: This is a short interrogative clause with the question word چرا at the front. نامدی is an archaic/poetic verb form here implying failure/not coming (treated as a verbal idea in this line) followed by the prepositional phrase نزد من ('to/near me' — in my presence). با خروش is an instrumental/adverbial phrase ('with a cry/uproar') modifying the action; word order (Q-word + verb + prepositional phrase + adverbial) is typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s111-g50: This clause continues the thought: خروش is the noun 'uproar/cry', توام is a predicative/adjectival participle meaning 'accompanied/combined' (i.e., joined with another uproar). چون here functions as 'as/when' linking the following verb رسیدی ('you arrived/reached' — simple past) to the prior state. به گوش is a directional phrase 'to the ear' (i.e., 'reached my ear' or 'came to hearing').
segments.s111-g51: سرم‌گر is a compact poetic conditional meaning roughly 'if it were my concern' or 'had I been (so disposed)'; ز خواب خوش آگه شدی is a causal/time phrase: ز خواب = 'from sleep', خواب خوش = 'pleasant/sweet sleep', آگه شدی uses آگه ('aware, roused') with شدی (past tense 'became') — together 'had been roused/aware from sweet sleep'. The conditional sense is signaled by سرم‌گر at the start.
segments.s111-g52: ترا is built from the enclitic 2nd-person suffix ت plus the object marker را (so it functions as 'you' in object-role: 'for you' or 'your'). جنگ با شیر is a noun phrase 'battle/fight with the lion'. کوته شدی is a compound predication where کوته 'short' is predicated by شدی ('became') — 'became short' = 'was cut short/was brief'. The whole clause states the result or consequence of the conditional above.
segments.s111-g53: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic poetic verb here expressing the sun's motion (literally 'struck/lifted'), and سر 'head' is used idiomatically with the verb to mean 'lift(ed) its head' = 'rose'. از تیره‌کوه (here split as از تیره کوه) is the source phrase 'from the dark/black mountain'. The word order (temporal clause followed by subject and verb) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s111-g54: This is a nominative clause about تهمتن (Tahmātan, an epithet/name). ز خواب خوش‌آمد = 'roused/from a pleasant sleep' (ز = 'from', خواب خوش = 'pleasant sleep', خوش‌آمد here functioning adjectivally), and ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary' or 'sore/distressed'. Together: 'Tahmatan, roused from sweet sleep, was weary.' Note the participial/nominal phrase construction common in poetry.
segments.s111-g55: تن رخش is a genitival/noun-noun phrase 'the body of Rakhsh' (Rakhsh = the horse). بسترد is a literary/archaic verb 'covered/veiled' (here meaning he covered or clothed the horse), و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', زین 'saddle', and برنهاد is the past form 'he placed/laid on' (برنهاد = 'set upon'). The two verb phrases describe successive, concrete actions: covering the horse and putting on the saddle.
segments.s111-g56: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of God' (here indicating source or cause), نیکی دهش is a compound noun meaning 'goodness/gift (of giving)' or 'gracious gift', کرد is the light/auxiliary verb 'did/made', and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' — together کرد یاد = 'he made remembrance' = 'he remembered' or 'he gave thanks/recalled'. The clause states a mental/ritual action following the physical ones.
segments.s111-g1: برون‌رفت پس پهلو نیمروز is a compact, somewhat archaic sequence: برون‌رفت (literally “went/issued forth”) is here a verbal/nominal action introducing what follows; پس is a temporal connector “then/after that”; پهلو نیمروز is a noun phrase with پهلو “flank/side” + نیمروز (the place-name Nimruz) used as an epithet — together functioning as the emerging party. Word order is Verb/phrase + temporal particle + noun phrase, typical of poetic compression.
segments.s111-g2: ز پیش پدر گرد گیتی فروز is a prepositional/attributive follow-up describing source and epithet: ز پیش پدر = “from/before (the) father” (ز = poetic 'from', پیش = 'before', پدر = 'father'); گرد گیتی‌فروز is an epithet attached after, literally “the (one) who makes the world shine” — here گرد is part of a heroic epithet and گیتی فروز (‘giti‑froz’) means “world‑illuminating/bright.” The whole phrase appends a descriptive tag to the emerging figure.
segments.s111-g3: دو روزه بیک روزه بگذاشتی is a compressed concessive/time clause: دو روزه (“for two days”) + بیک روزه (archaic contraction of بهِ یک روزه, “as one day”) + بگذاشتی (past, 2nd‑singular: “you made/left/treated”). Grammatically it means “you made (or treated) two days as a single day,” with بیک functioning as an older fused form of به یک.
segments.s111-g4: شب تیره را روز پنداشتی is a simple transitive clause: شب تیره (the dark night) + را (definite object marker) + روز پنداشتی (‘you thought/took for day’). پنداشتی is past 2nd‑singular of پنداشتن, here meaning “to suppose/consider” rather than mere imaginative belief.
segments.s111-g5: بدین سان همی رخش ببرید راه is an idiomatic poetic clause: بدین سان (in/‘by this manner’) + همی (an older continuous/emphatic particle) + رخش (his steed) + ببرید راه (literally ‘cut the way’ — ببرید here is a past/preterite-form meaning ‘cut through/cleared’). Word order places the manner first, then the subject and the verbal phrase, typical in epic diction.
segments.s111-g6: بتابنده روز و شبان سیاه is a two‑part adjectival phrase modifying راه/رخش: بتابنده روز = ‘shining by day’ (بتابنده = present‑participle adjective ‘that shines’) + و شبان سیاه = ‘and black by night’ (شبان here functions poetically to link ‘night’ quality). The phrase gives contrasting day/night appearances.
segments.s111-g7: تنش چون خورش جست و آمد به شور is a sequence of actions: تنش = تن + ـش (his body), چون = ‘like/as’, خورش here is a poetic image (contextually ‘like the sun’ or an intense thing) and جست is past ‘leapt/sprung’; و آمد به شور = ‘and came into shor’ (شور = passion/tumult). The structure is subject (possessed noun) + simile + verb(s) in the past tense.
segments.s111-g8: یکی دشت پیش آمدش پر ز گور is a locative/visual clause: یکی دشت = ‘a certain plain’ (یکی as ‘a certain’), پیش آمدش = ‘came before him/appeared’ (آمدش = came to him/appeared), پر ز گور = ‘full of graves’ (پر = full (of) + ز = ‘of/from’ + گور = graves). Word order is determiner + place, then the verb with clitic object, then the descriptive prepositional phrase.
segments.s111-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیدار شد is a two-word past construction: بیدار (awake) + شد (become) = 'awoke'/'became awake'. رستم is the proper name (subject) and تیزچنگ is an epithet/name attached to Rustom (placed after his name here). Word order: conjunction + predicate 'bídar shod' then the named subject with epithet.
segments.s111-g42: جهان دید is literally 'saw the world' (here functioning as the verb phrase 'he saw'); بر before شیر means 'in front of / toward / before' so the clause means he saw (before him) a شیر. تاریک و تنگ are coordinating adjectives describing that lion — 'dark' and 'close/narrow' (tung here conveys closeness or confined appearance).
segments.s111-g43: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech). با + رخش is the addressee 'to/with Rakhsh' (Rakhsh = the steed). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and هوشیار is the vocative adjective 'watchful/alert'. The whole segment: 'Thus he said to Rakhsh, "O watchful one".'
segments.s111-g44: The initial که here begins a relative/indirect question clause 'who/that'. گفتت is the past 'said/told you' (گفت + enclitic ـت = 'said to you'). The second که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported content: با شیر کن کارزار = 'to make/engage in battle with the lion' where کن is the verb 'do' (imperative/infinitival root used to mean 'engage in / make') and کارزار is 'battle'. The clause asks 'who told you to fight the lion?'.
segments.s111-g45: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. شدی (past of شدن) with the past participle کشته forms a resultative: 'you became killed' = 'you are killed' / 'should you be slain'. در + چنگ اوی = 'in his grip/claw' with اوی an archaic possessive 'his'. Overall: 'If you are killed in his claws...' (classical conditional wording).
segments.s111-g46: This clause has an implied copula: من (I) + این گرز و این مغفر (this mace and this covering/helmet). جنگجوی follows and functions adjectivally/possessively ('of the warrior' or 'warrior's'), a common poetic order without an explicit ezafe. So the meaning is 'I am this mace and this warrior's helmet' (the verb 'am' is omitted but understood).
segments.s111-g47: چگونه is the interrogative 'how'. کشیدی is 2nd-person singular past of کشیدن 'to pull/bring/draw' — here 'did you bring / take'. به marks the destination 'to/into', and مازندران is the place name (Mazandaran). The clause asks how the addressee transported or brought something into Mazandaran.
segments.s111-g48: This is a noun phrase listing the things brought: کمند 'lasso/snare' modified by کیانی 'Kayanian/royal' (adjective after the noun in poetic order), و 'and', گرز 'mace' modified by گران 'heavy/valuable' (postpositive adjective). It thus names 'the Kayanian lasso and the heavy mace.'
segments.s111-g9: This is a compact clause: 'یکی' ('a certain/one' or 'at once' in epic diction) is a fronting adverbial/subject marker, followed by the direct object marked by 'را' (رخش را = Rakhsh as definite object). 'تیز بنمود' is a past causative/performative: بنمود (past of نمودن) here means 'made/caused to be' so تیز بنمود = 'made (it) swift'. 'ران' (hindquarter) follows as an adjunct indicating how/where he urged the horse (literally 'spurred at the haunch').
segments.s111-g10: This clause states a change of state with شد (become). تگ (here = 'narrow/tight') is predicated of گور ('onager', the wild ass); the preposition-like از introduces the cause/reason (از = 'because of' / 'on account of'), followed by تگِ او ('his tightness/flank') and the adjective گران ('heavy'). The order is typical Persian: predicate (تگ گور شد) then causal phrase (از تگ او گران).
segments.s111-g11: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing means/agents: 'کمند' (lasso) and then 'پی رخش' (پی = 'in pursuit of' or 'following' + رخش = Rakhsh) and finally 'رستم سوار' ('Rostam the rider' with سوار as an appositive/epithet). The nouns are juxtaposed with و as a simple connective.
segments.s111-g12: نیابد is the negative present/present-subjunctive form of یابیدن ('does not / cannot find' or 'may not find'); here it functions as the main verb meaning 'cannot find' or 'will find none'. ازو = 'from him' / 'against him' indicates target/source, followed by the coordinated objects 'دام و دد' ('trap and enemy'). زینهار is a vocative/exclamatory admonition 'take heed / beware' attached at the end.
segments.s111-g13: کمند کیانی = 'the Kayanian (royal) lasso' (کیانی is an epithet/attributive following the noun). بینداخت is past tense 'threw/cast' (from انداختن) and takes شیر ('lion') as its object; the object-marker را is omitted in this terse epic style.
segments.s111-g14: به حلقه درآورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'brought/dragged into the ring': به = 'into/towards', حلقه = 'ring/loop', درآورد (here) = 'brought in / drew in' (past). گور دلیر is the object phrase 'the brave onager' (گور = onager; دلیر = brave) placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s111-g15: کشید و بیفگند are two past verbs in sequence: کشید (past of کشیدن) = 'he pulled/drew' and بیفگند (past of افگندن) = 'he threw/cast'. Together they describe two consecutive actions done to گور ('the onager'), which follows the verbs in the line; آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time'.
segments.s111-g16: بیامد = 'came' (past). برش here is morphologically بر + ش (the preposition 'upon' + enclitic pronoun 'him') and therefore means 'came upon him' — it is not the nominal 'برش' = 'cut/slice'. چون = 'like/as' introduces a simile, followed by هژبر دمان, a compound name/epithet (Hežbar Daman) used as the point of comparison ('like Hejbar Daman').

sentences.s111-s13: He made a sleeping bed upon the reed‑bed and judged the place safe in case of danger.
sentences.s111-s14: In that reed‑bed was a thicket of lions, which (poetically described) no elephant‑companion sent greeting from the reeds.
sentences.s111-s15: When a sentry/passer had gone by a pace, the fierce lion came back toward his lair, brave.
sentences.s111-s16: On the reed he saw an elephant asleep; beside it he saw a distressed horse.
sentences.s111-s17: First he said to the horse that it must be broken; when I want to mount, it will come into my hand by itself.
sentences.s111-s18: Toward Rakhsh the Radiant the hem/skirts rose up; at that moment Rakhsh seethed like fire.
sentences.s111-s19: He seized with both hands and struck him on the head; that same sharp fang sank into his back.
sentences.s111-s20: He kept striking on that ground until he tore him apart; thereby he left the demon helpless and without recourse.
sentences.s111-s9: With a fire-arrow from his arrows he kindled a blaze; earth, chaff, and firewood burned at it.
sentences.s111-s10: Thereupon the keen fire seared him; afterwards it made him skinless and lifeless.
sentences.s111-s11: He ate and threw away its bones; this was the cauldron and this was the table.
sentences.s111-s12: He took the bridle from Rakhsh's head; he saw (him) grazing and left him in the meadow.
sentences.s111-s25: Why did you not come to me with a cry? My uproar was joined with yours when it reached my ear.
sentences.s111-s26: Had I been roused from my pleasant sleep, your fight with the lion would have been short.
sentences.s111-s27: When the sun rose from the dark mountain, Tahmatan, roused from pleasant sleep, was weary.
sentences.s111-s28: He covered Rakhsh's body and set the saddle upon him; he remembered God's gracious gift.
sentences.s111-s1: Then the flank of Nimruz came forth; from before his father came the world-illuminating one.
sentences.s111-s2: You treated two days as one day; you took the dark night for day.
sentences.s111-s3: Thus his steed cut the road, shining by day and black by night.
sentences.s111-s4: His body leapt like the sun and came into a frenzy; a certain plain before him appeared full of graves.
sentences.s111-s21: When Rostam Tizchang awoke, he saw before him a dark, close-at-hand lion.
sentences.s111-s22: Thus he said to his Rakhsh, 'O watchful one — who told you to make war with the lion?'.
sentences.s111-s23: If you are slain in his grip, I am this mace and this warrior's helmet.
sentences.s111-s24: How did you bring/take into Mazandaran the Kayanian lasso and the heavy mace?
sentences.s111-s5: At once he urged Rakhsh to speed with the haunch; the onager's flank became constricted, and because of his flank it grew heavy.
sentences.s111-s6: No lasso, no pursuit of Rakhsh, nor Rostam the rider can find a trap or foe against him — take heed!
sentences.s111-s7: The Kayanian lasso was cast (or the royal lasso was thrown) and caught the lion; it drew the brave onager into the ring.
sentences.s111-s8: He pulled and flung the onager; at that moment someone came upon him like Hejbar Daman.
